nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, sheltered inshore waters over sandy, rubble, muddy, and weedy substrates; seagrass beds, coastal reef margins, and estuarine margins. Primarily subtidal, resting motionless on the bottom and swaying to mimic dead leaves or drifting debris. Collected intertidally to ~80 m but typically found above 20 m.

decorPreferences: fine sand or mixed sand-rubble substrate for resting and camouflage
seagrass or robust macroalgae clusters for cover and leaf-mimicry context
low open rockwork with shaded overhangs
subdued lighting to support nocturnal feeding schedule

diet.notes: Obligate ambush predator; accepts only live prey in virtually all captive records. Live ghost shrimp, grass shrimp, small feeder fish (guppies, mollies), and live polychaete worms are the mainstay. Some individuals can be slowly transitioned to freshly killed or thawed whole shrimp dangled on a feeding stick, but most specimens refuse non-moving food indefinitely. Feed every 2–4 days; overfeeding causes rapid water-quality deterioration. Cannibalistic — do not house with conspecifics unless a proven pair.

goodWith: robust mid-water fish too large to swallow (SG-tolerant monos, large scats)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed whole
aggressive fish (groupers, moray eels, sharks, frogfishes) that stress or injure the waspfish
conspecifics unless confirmed mated pair introduced simultaneously
any fish requiring full freshwater conditions

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Ablabys taenianotus; shows characteristic tall dorsal crest and leaf-like body profile.

summary: The Cockatoo Waspfish is an Indo-Pacific estuarine ambush predator that mimics a drifting dead leaf. Its venomous dorsal spines, live-feeder-only diet, and brackish-to-marine water requirements make it strictly a specialist species — not a true freshwater fish despite occasional mislabeling in the trade.
careNotes: Ablabys taenianotus is sold in the aquarium trade under the names Waspfish, Cockatoo Waspfish, and Leaf Fish, and is occasionally mislabeled as a freshwater species. It is not a freshwater fish. In the wild it inhabits sheltered inshore coastal and estuarine zones across the Indo-Pacific (Andaman Sea to Fiji, Japan to Australia), tolerating low-end brackish conditions near estuarine outflows but requiring at minimum mid-brackish to low-marine salinity (SG 1.010–1.025) for long-term health. Full marine conditions (SG 1.020–1.025) are strongly preferred and should be the keeper’s target. A tank of at least 150 L with a fine sand or sand-rubble bottom and seagrass or macroalgae for cover is required. Flow should be gentle; the species naturally inhabits sheltered, low-surge environments. Lighting should be dim or on a reverse-daylight schedule since the species is primarily nocturnal, becoming active and hunting after dark. The dominant challenge in captivity is feeding: specimens almost universally refuse non-living food on acquisition, and many never accept anything other than live prey. Live ghost shrimp, grass shrimp, and small feeder fish are the practical staples; a patient keeper may slowly wean some individuals onto thawed whole shrimp animated on a feeding stick. Feeding attempts 2–4 times per week are typically sufficient; the species has a slow metabolism and can fast for days between meals. Water quality must remain excellent — nitrogenous waste from live-prey feeding accumulates quickly. SAFETY: the elongated dorsal fin spines are venomous and capable of delivering a severely painful, medically significant sting comparable to a scorpionfish envenomation. Never place bare hands in the tank without visual confirmation of the fish’s exact position. Use thick puncture-resistant gloves and/or a specimen container for any maintenance requiring hand entry. Seek medical attention for any confirmed sting. This species should be kept singly in a species-dedicated setup; it is cannibalistic and will consume any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth.
breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Spawning behaviour in the wild involves pair formation; FishBase notes the species may be found singly or in pairs. Captive reproduction is not achievable under current hobby conditions and is not a realistic goal. Specimens in the trade are wild-caught.

primary.name: FishBase — Ablabys taenianotus
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biological reference. Confirmed: max 15 cm TL, depth 0–80 m, preferred temperature 23.5–29°C (mean 28.1°C), native range Eastern Indian Ocean and Western Pacific (Andaman Sea to Fiji, north to Japan, south to Australia), habitat shallow subtidal sand-rubble-weed, nocturnal, venomous, family Tetrarogidae, order Scorpaeniformes. Used as authoritative source for size, range, and taxonomy.

additional.name: Reeflings — Cockatoo Waspfish Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Aquarium trade care guide. Confirmed adult size 15 cm, temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.022–1.025, minimum tank 100 L, carnivore diet (live/frozen shrimp and small fish), difficult care level, venomous spines, solitary, not captive bred.

additional.name: Fishes of Australia — Ablabys taenianotus
additional.notes: Regional taxonomic reference for Australian distribution. Confirmed max 15 cm TL, depth 0–80 m (typically to 20 m), habitat sandy/rubble/muddy/weedy sheltered inshore reefs, venomous spines. Provided Australian distribution detail (NW Cape WA to Sydney NSW, Christmas Island, Lord Howe, Norfolk Island) used to inform countries list and range narrative.

additional.name: Aquatic Community — Cockatoo Waspfish
additional.notes: Hobbyist care reference. Confirmed 15 cm adult size, diet (fish, crabs, polychaete worms, shrimp, jellyfish, medusae), cannibalistic behaviour, sensitivity to water quality, avoidance of sharks/groupers/frogfishes/morays, seagrass and sand habitat requirement. Noted as marine species.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ablabys taenianotus
additional.notes: Cross-reference for taxonomy, size, native range, and behaviour. Confirmed Tetrarogidae family, tropical West Pacific range, depth 0.6–18.2 m (shallower estimate), seagrass and rubble habitat, benthic nocturnal ambush predator, venomous dorsal spines, diet of small shrimp and crustaceans. Habitat depth cross-checked against FishBase (0–80 m) and Fishes of Australia (0–80 m); broader range preferred.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Wild pairs spawn seasonally; aquarium reproduction is not feasible and no protocols exist.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: VENOMOUS DORSAL SPINES capable of severe envenomation. Indo-Pacific brackish-marine; not true freshwater. Live-feeder only. Specialist single-fish setup with handler-safety protocols.
